headline: Jeannette Rankin, First Woman in Congress, Born, 1880
body:
Now, folks, if you ever had the pleasure of observing a particularly industrious hen, clucking and fussing over her brood, you'd have a fair notion of Montana in the early years of the twentieth century, only instead of chicks, it was counties, and instead of clucking, it was a legislative hullabaloo that would make a brass band sound like a whisper. Yes, indeed, a veritable county-splitting craze had gripped the Treasure State, a feverish division of land and loyalties, and on the ninth day of December, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and thirteen, another chick, or rather, another county, pecked its way out of the shell of Custer County: Fallon County.

It was a time when the railroad, that iron horse of progress, was carving its way across the vast, untamed prairies, bringing with it the promise of prosperity and the clamor of new settlements. The Milwaukee Road, a grand enterprise if ever there was one, had laid its tracks and, in its wisdom, established a little burg that was first christened Lorraine, after some railroad official's daughter, no doubt. But soon enough, the name Baker took hold, a name as plain and honest as the folks who settled there, and it was this very Baker that would become the beating heart, the county seat, of the newly formed Fallon County.

Now, the naming of a county is a serious business, and they didn't just pull a name out of a hat, though some might suspect as much given the sheer number of counties popping up like mushrooms after a spring rain. No, Fallon County was bestowed with the moniker of Benjamin O'Fallon, a man of some historical note, a nephew, mind you, of none other than Captain William Clark himself, that intrepid explorer who, with Lewis, had traipsed across these very lands a century prior. O'Fallon, an Indian agent in his day, was a figure of the frontier, a man whose name carried the scent of exploration and the early dealings between nations, a fitting patron for a county born of a new era.

There was, of course, a bit of a tussle over where the county seat ought to be. Ekalaka, a town with a name that rolls off the tongue like a good tall tale, had its proponents, but in the end, the iron horse had its way, and Baker, with its Milwaukee Road connections, won the day. It was a common enough drama in those parts, a contest of wills and geography, often decided by the whistle of a train or the promise of a new depot. The legislative halls in Helena, I imagine, buzzed with the arguments, the petitions, the earnest pleas of citizens who believed their patch of Montana dirt was the rightful center of the universe. One can almost hear the impassioned speeches, the thumping of gavels, as the boundaries were drawn and redrawn, each line on the map representing a triumph for some, a disappointment for others. But when the ink was dry and the proclamations made, Fallon County stood, a testament to the relentless spirit of expansion and the peculiar American habit of carving up the wilderness into neat, administrative squares, all in the name of progress and, dare I say, a bit of local pride.
